body.u: In the summer of 1978 I left school with only half the ‘O’levels I had hoped to get. But didn’t stop my chances of getting into the army as an officer. I was nervous at first when I joined but I soon got over it as I seemed to fit in so well. Those years in the army were some of the best in my life, I met some real good mates and always seemed to {illegible} a laugh, but the army had it’sits disadvantages with Northern Ireland and such places. The fives years in the army seemed more like a five weeks holiday, it was in the last year that I met my wife at a party on leave. She was two years younger than me and we got along fine. She made my days off something really to look forward to. It was her that prompted me to leave the army since we had become engaged.

When I left I went into the timber trade early learning the pros and cons of running a business. By the time I had learnt enough to move on to another firm as a representative, to learn how to sell your product, I was married with a house in Sittingbourne and a baby on the way. We had a lot of good times in that house and many memories of the children growing for badies to children.

After eight years as a representative I felt confident enough to go into business on my own. I brought a small piece of land in Queenborough and moved back to the Island where I myself had been brought up.

Money was short in my first few years of business but it soon picked up and began to make instead of lose. The children, 2 boys 1 girl, were now tall (the eldest bigger than me), and Susan, my daughter was beginning to show signs of being a woman and demanding her own way.

The business grew, and life went on. The children began one by one to leave home. Lynn, my wife, and I were beginning to slow down and with bussinessbusiness booming and the children goongone we decided to move to a new home. This was more luxurious but was never quite the same as our last home.

I began to leave work more and more untilluntil it was merely a once a week visit, but I received the same income. My wife and I saw less and less of other people but we had our children and our grandchildren to keep us company.

I was deeply shocked when the communist party gained power and all bussinessbusiness taken away from people who had work had to gain them. I lost my firm and was dismissed as a director. I was forced sell my house and buy something smaller but I don’t really mind I still have Lynn and our children. I now don’t care what happens as long as me and Lynn can finish our days together.
